content: Hi - how do I turn this off? It looks like we suddenly turned it on automatically for everyone, and I really would rather not have this responding to my work generally.

For the time being, I'm just going to block this. The vast majority of the feedback this is generating is completely wrong / borderline non-sense. But please maybe make these things opt-in instead of just unleashing AI tools on everyone without asking people in the future?

Lyude said:

For the time being, I'm just going to block this. The vast majority of the feedback this is generating is completely wrong / borderline non-sense. But please maybe make these things opt-in instead of just unleashing AI tools on everyone without asking people in the future?

I don't think anyone here is affiliated with sashiko.
I tohught it was ojeda's, since it seems to cc them on all of the emails
But if someone else knows who I can ask to turn it into an opt-in I'd certainly appreciate it.
It's maintainer's decision currently: https://github.com/sashiko-dev/sashiko/pull/112. Not sure if there're opt-out mechanisms. You could probably just add rules to filter them?
I'll leave a response there asking them to switch it to opt-in then.

It's maintainer's decision currently

By enabling this for the rust-for-linux list it actually becomes the opposite, as the rust-for-linux list matchs all Rust code in the kernel.

So, effectively, it overrules other maintainers when they decide not to send mails to contributors for now.

If we want this for the core Rust infrastructure we can have a separate list for Rust core infrastructure only (as we have discussed in the past) and enable it there.

I'd still really rather this be opt-in anywhere it is available. If people want to deal with going through all the false positives and bring up genuine issues it finds on people's patches that's totally fine, but I don't see why that onus should be on the actual patch author given the really high number of false positives it gives.

Danilo Krummrich said:

By enabling this for the rust-for-linux list it actually becomes the opposite, as the rust-for-linux list matchs all Rust code in the kernel.

Just to clarify, I'm not saying this is a policy issue, but a technical limitation, which can go both directions -- a subsystem that does not want contributors to receive those mails could also disable it for those who want them when multiple lists are addressed.

In general my experience has been that sashiko can provide very valuable feedback. However, one reason why a subsystem might not want to have mails sent to the contributor is that it might lead to the contributor acting on feedback without further discussion / involvement from everyone else the patch is addressed at.

One possible way to address this could be an explicit note in replies that mentions something along the lines of "If this report did provide feedback you consider acting on, please raise the topic on-list first.".

Anyways, this thread is about a different topic, so I don't want to hijack it any further. :)

For me it's really just a matter of I think if people want to use AI tooling, they should also be responsible for the overhead of verifying its output - and having it respond publically on the mailing list just feels like it's moving that overhead onto everyone
Especially since we already seem to have tooling that lets us view what sashiko would say in response to anyone's patches on the mailing list anyway

As long as it could find actual bugs (with reasonable false positives rate), people would want to use it to improve quality of codebase. I don't think pushing to have maintainers to verify the output of Sashiko instead of sending it to the author is a good thing, given that they're already busy and overworked.

You don't ask maintainers to verify that Clippy is happy with your code. It has it's fair share of false positive too. Sashiko is just another tool like this.

Perhaps false positive rate of Sashiko is a bit too high today, but that should be the direction of improvement rather than a complete dismissal.

FWIW, a lot of time I spent last cycle reviewing was pointing out things that will be easily caught by AI review tool by not otherwise available by existing deterministic tools. Missing inline, wrong import format, missing invariant comment, etc. It would save everyone's time if that sort of issues are reported and fixed early.

Perhaps you patch series has a big version number so it doesn't have many of these issues now; but I think having them enabled for v1/v2 would be a net benefit.
